column2: San Diego Gas & Electric Company, with seven other participants, proposes to build the Sundesert Nuclear Plant—two pressurized-water reactors of about 974 MWe each—on a 2,859-hectare site on the Palo Verde Mesa in Riverside County, California, roughly 9 km west of the Colorado River and 26 km southwest of Blythe. Cooling water (up to about 2 m³/sec) would come from the Palo Verde Outfall Drain, with mechanical-draft cooling towers and a lined evaporation basin so that no thermal or chemical effluents reach surface waters. The project also includes about 618 km of new 500-kV and 230-kV transmi…
column7: The core project description, site parameters, and major alternatives are well-supported by the cited pages. However, several specific quantitative claims (coal being 4-12x more harmful, 2,400-4,000 …

column2: The plant site is in a sparsely populated desert area near the small communities of Palo Verde and Ripley (each about 200–300 residents), with Blythe (the main service center) about 26 km to the northeast; only about 16 permanent residents lived within 3–5 km in 1970. A peak construction workforce of roughly 2,800 and about 200 permanent operations workers would strain housing, schools, hospitals, and water and sewer services in the Palo Verde Valley. The transmission corridors cross or pass near many additional communities—including Indio, Coachella, Palm Springs, Banning, Beaumont, Temecula…

The NRC staff evaluated alternatives to the project including purchased power, postponing retirement or upgrading of existing units, and other generating sources such as coal, oil, gas, geothermal, hydroelectric, solar, wind, biomass, fuel cells, and fusion, concluding that only a coal-fired plant was a reasonable alternative—and that coal would be 4–12 times more harmful to human health than the nuclear option. Fourteen candidate site areas were screened, with Palo Verde North, Blythe, Milpitas Wash, Parker Valley, and Camp Pendleton receiving closer review. Cooling-system options compared i…

Plant construction would disturb about 300 hectares on site and 101 hectares offsite, with short-term turbidity, siltation, and possible mercury resuspension where intake structures are built in the Palo Verde Outfall Drain, Oxbow Lake, and the Colorado River. Cooling-tower salt drift could raise soil salinity enough to kill creosote bush and bursage on up to 2,400–4,000 hectares around the plant, and water withdrawals could expose bank habitat used by the endangered Yuma clapper rail. The 618 km of transmission corridors would clear hundreds of hectares and cause high visual, recreational, b…

Community surveys along the corridors found that visual intrusion was the most widely shared concern, followed by worries about radio and television interference (especially among elderly residents in remote desert communities), interference with crop dusting and irrigation, and effects on future community growth. The Morongo, Pechanga, and Pala Indian Reservations raised concerns about land-use fragmentation, archaeological disturbance, and health and safety, and backcountry users and agencies strongly opposed routings through Anza-Borrego State Park and other outstanding recreational areas.…
